build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: Players alternate playing one card per turn from hand into one of three rows (melee, ranged, siege). Each card has a strength value that adds to the row's total. The side with higher total strength at round's end wins. Cards are placed by clicking/dragging into the appropriate row. Score 0 if there are no rows, if cards have no strength values, or if placement is not interactive. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

The player can pass at any time instead of playing a card, locking in their current strength. Once both players pass, the round ends. Matches are best-of-3 rounds. A round tracker shows current standing (wins per side). The bluffing tension of when to pass is the core strategic decision. Score 0 if passing is not possible, if rounds do not end on mutual pass, or if there is no best-of-3 structure. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Cards have special abilities beyond raw strength: Spy (played on opponent's side, draws 2 cards), Medic (resurrects from discard), Weather (reduces a row to 1 strength each), Commander's Horn (doubles a row's strength), Decoy (returns a played card to hand). At least 5 ability types exist with distinct visual effects. Score 0 if cards have no abilities, if fewer than 5 types exist, or if abilities have no visual effect. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

At least 3 faction decks with different card pools, strategies, and faction abilities. Each faction has a unique visual identity (banner colour, card border style). The player selects their faction before a match. Score 0 if there is only one faction, if factions share the same cards, or if there is no faction selection. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Each faction has at least 15 unique cards with different strength values, row assignments, and abilities. Cards have names, illustrated art, and clear stat displays. The total card pool across all factions is at least 45 cards. Score 0 if factions have fewer than 15 cards each, if cards have no art, or if the total pool is under 45. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

A deck builder lets the player construct a deck of exactly 25 cards from their faction plus neutral cards. The builder shows all available cards with filtering and sorting. Deck composition affects strategy. Score 0 if there is no deck builder, if deck size is not enforced, or if the player cannot choose their cards. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

A campaign mode with at least 5 AI opponents of increasing difficulty and unique deck strategies. Winning matches earns new cards for the player's collection. A world map or progression screen shows campaign progress. Score 0 if there is no campaign, if there are fewer than 5 opponents, or if winning earns nothing. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

The battle screen clearly shows: three rows per side with cards placed, row strength totals, overall strength totals, the player's hand, round tracker, and pass button. All elements are readable and non-overlapping at 1280x720. Row totals update immediately when cards are played. Score 0 if any core element is missing or if totals are not shown. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Card abilities produce visible effects: Weather cards change the row's appearance, Spy cards visibly move to the opponent's side, Medic resurrections show a card rising from the discard pile, Commander's Horn shows a strength-doubling animation. The player can understand what happened from visual cues. Score 0 if abilities trigger invisibly, or if there is no visual distinction between ability types. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The full game loop is connected: Title -> Faction/deck select -> Match (play cards, pass, rounds) -> Match result -> Campaign progression or return to menu. The player can experience every node without restarting. Score 0 if any transition in the chain is missing or broken. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

A title screen is present and styled: it shows the game name in a custom or themed font, has at minimum a play button that is interactive and transitions to the game, and uses a non-default background (medieval war table, faction banners, or battlefield) — not plain Godot grey or a solid-colour fill. Score 0 if the game launches directly into gameplay with no title screen, or if the start button is non-functional. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Overall art-style coherence: every visible element — cards, board, faction banners, UI panels, weather effects — belongs to a single consistent visual language. The palette is medieval/fantasy themed (rich colours, parchment tones); no clashing styles. Score 0 if the screen mixes incompatible styles, or if elements are obviously unthemed placeholder shapes. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Cards have illustrated character art, faction-coloured borders, and readable stat text; the board has a textured surface with row labels; faction banners are illustrated; UI panels use styled medieval-themed widgets. Score 0 if cards are plain text boxes, if the board is a solid colour, or if UI is unstyled defaults. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Animation continuity and polish: card placement, ability activation effects, weather overlay, strength total updates, round-end announcements, pass animations, and screen transitions each play as smooth multi-frame tweens or sprite animations. No element snaps to its new state in a single frame. Transitions use fades or slides. Score 0 if any of these moments is instantaneous, or if transitions are hard cuts. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
